summary:
The anterior ethmoidal artery commonly supplies the nasal septum; thus, it can be ruled out from options where other listed arteries are considered for their less direct or consistent involvement in this region. \(\blacksquare\) Sphenopalatine, greater palatine, and superior labial arteries do not predominantly supply the nasal septum. 

The final summary focuses clearly on identifying non-suppliers based directly upon anatomical roles rather than potential indirect contributions via collaterals under specific conditions. This aligns closely with standard medical knowledge regarding primary vascular sources within head structures such as those surrounding the nose and its partitions (\(septum\)). Thus:

\[ \textbf{\Large Summary}: None of } \text{sphenopalatine}, \textgreater{} \text{palatine}, \text{or }\text{superior}\,\text{labial\ textit{typically}} \\ 
\text{supply the nasal septum.\normalsize} \] Agent2 summarized by focusing first on eliminating incorrect answers related to known anatomic functions away from providing blood flow into specified zones versus assuming minor auxiliary support routes exist without clear evidence supporting significant reliance thereupon across general patient populations undergoing routine diagnostic evaluations involving neurovascular assessments pertinent here concerning facial structure innervation patterns. Hence,
\[ \boxed{\text{sphenopalatine, greater palatine, and superior labial arteries do not supply the nasal septum}.}
\]
